summary: Muratori describes a lecture he gave at Busan Indie Connect 2018 about the player movement system he developed for The Witness. The lecture details novel algorithms that provide real-time, precomputation-free, provably robust player movement. It follows up on his earlier 2012 'Walk Monster' article about making player movement more robust. The post is primarily a reference page for the lecture video, noting that the original recording had severe audio issues but an improved version using simultaneous translation recordings was later created.
key_points: The Witness's final movement system used novel algorithms for real-time, precomputation-free, provably robust player movement
The lecture covers motivation, implementation overview, and practical examples from both the game and editor
This work evolved over at least six years from the initial 2012 Walk Monster article to the 2018 lecture
The original recording had severe audio problems due to the venue's inexperienced A/V setup
money_quotes: The final movement system shipped in The Witness used several novel algorithms to provide real-time, precomputation-free, provably robust player movement.
This lecture gives the motivation behind the system, an overview of its implementation, and examples of how it is used in both the game and the editor.
key_insight: The Witness's player movement system was solved through novel algorithms that achieved provably robust, real-time movement without precomputation, representing years of iterative development from initial problem identification to final shipped solution.
